original_favourites: J.R.R. Tolkien — The Lord of the Rings
Ursula K. Le Guin — The Left Hand of Darkness
Ursula K. Le Guin — The Dispossessed
Ursula K. Le Guin — A Wizard of Earthsea
Ursula K. Le Guin — The Word for World is Forest

genres.label: Epic and secondary-world fantasy
genres.sub: For when you want the sweep and depth of Middle-earth all over again

genres.label: Hard sci-fi and near-future survival
genres.sub: For when you want The Martian's mix of ingenuity and tension

genres.label: Alternative history and political thriller
genres.sub: For when you want the shadowy what-ifs of Robert Harris

genres.label: Richly researched historical fiction
genres.sub: For when you want Pompeii's sense of a vanished world made vivid

genres.label: Philosophical and anthropological sci-fi
genres.sub: For when you want Le Guin's probing of society, gender and power

genres.label: Immersive world-building and mythopoeic fiction
genres.sub: For when you want Le Guin's and Tolkien's crafted languages, maps and legends

seed_books.title: The Name of the Wind
seed_books.author: Patrick Rothfuss
seed_books.why: Like Tolkien, Rothfuss builds a deeply detailed secondary world with its own history and magic system. The prose is lyrical and the storytelling self-aware in ways that reward patient readers.

seed_books.title: The Sword of Kaigen
seed_books.author: M.L. Wang
seed_books.why: A self-contained epic fantasy with the emotional weight of Tolkien and a non-Western setting that feels as fully realised as Middle-earth. War, myth and family collide brilliantly.

seed_books.title: Assassin's Apprentice
seed_books.author: Robin Hobb
seed_books.why: Robin Hobb's Farseer Trilogy shares Tolkien's moral seriousness and long-game storytelling, following a royal bastard in a deeply imagined coastal kingdom.

seed_books.title: Tigana
seed_books.author: Guy Gavriel Kay
seed_books.why: Kay studied under Tolkien's son Christopher and helped edit The Silmarillion, and Tigana channels that same elegiac grandeur into a story about memory, identity and lost nations.

seed_books.title: The Calculating Stars
seed_books.author: Mary Robinette Kowal
seed_books.why: Like The Martian, this is rigorous, problem-solving sci-fi centred on a determined protagonist — here a female astronaut racing to get humanity off an imperilled Earth.

seed_books.title: Seveneves
seed_books.author: Neal Stephenson
seed_books.why: Stephenson's orbital survival epic shares Andy Weir's love of real physics and engineering detail, scaling the lone-survivor ingenuity of The Martian to all of humanity.

seed_books.title: The Long Way to a Small, Angry Planet
seed_books.author: Becky Chambers
seed_books.why: Chambers blends the warmth and crew-resourcefulness of The Martian with hopeful, character-driven interstellar travel. A gentle antidote that never sacrifices plausibility.

seed_books.title: Delta-v
seed_books.author: Daniel Suarez
seed_books.why: A near-future asteroid-mining thriller with the same technical authenticity and seat-of-your-pants problem-solving that makes The Martian so compulsive.

seed_books.title: SS-GB
seed_books.author: Len Deighton
seed_books.why: Like Robert Harris's Fatherland, this is a landmark Nazi-victory alternate history — a London under occupation seen through the eyes of a murder detective navigating moral compromise.

seed_books.title: The Man in the High Castle
seed_books.author: Philip K. Dick
seed_books.why: The founding text of the Axis-won-war subgenre that Harris revisited in Fatherland, dense with paranoia and questions about what history and identity actually mean.

seed_books.title: The Spy Who Came in from the Cold
seed_books.author: John le Carré
seed_books.why: Le Carré's Cold War espionage shares Robert Harris's taste for moral ambiguity, institutional betrayal and the grinding machinery of intelligence services.

seed_books.title: Munich
seed_books.author: Robert Harris
seed_books.why: Already a Harris fan? His Munich is sometimes overlooked — a taut diplomatic thriller set over the four days of the 1938 conference, as tightly wound as any of his best work.

seed_books.title: HHhH
seed_books.author: Laurent Binet
seed_books.why: Binet reconstructs the 1942 assassination attempt on Reinhard Heydrich with the same obsessive archival fidelity as Robert Harris, while brilliantly interrogating the act of historical fiction itself.

seed_books.title: The Pillars of the Earth
seed_books.author: Ken Follett
seed_books.why: Like Pompeii, this is meticulously researched immersive history — a cathedral-building epic set in 12th-century England that makes the medieval world physically present.

seed_books.title: The Name of the Rose
seed_books.author: Umberto Eco
seed_books.why: Eco's medieval monastery murder mystery combines Harris's thriller plotting with extraordinary scholarly depth — a book that rewards rereading as richly as any Tolkien text.

seed_books.title: Imperium
seed_books.author: Robert Harris
seed_books.why: If you haven't read Harris's Cicero trilogy, Imperium is an essential companion to Pompeii — the same vivid ancient world, this time seen through the eyes of Rome's greatest orator.

seed_books.title: The Female Man
seed_books.author: Joanna Russ
seed_books.why: Like Le Guin's The Left Hand of Darkness, this is a radical feminist thought-experiment in sci-fi form, sending the same woman across four parallel worlds with different gender realities.

seed_books.title: Babel-17
seed_books.author: Samuel R. Delany
seed_books.why: Delany shares Le Guin's fascination with language as a shaper of thought and society — this slim, brilliant novel asks whether a weapon can be a language and a language a weapon.

seed_books.title: The City & The City
seed_books.author: China Miéville
seed_books.why: Miéville's novel about two cities occupying the same space asks Le Guin-style questions about perception, power and ideology, wrapped in a gripping noir detective plot.

seed_books.title: Always Coming Home
seed_books.author: Ursula K. Le Guin
seed_books.why: One of Le Guin's most experimental and underread works — a future California tribe's entire culture rendered as archaeology, poetry and story. Unmissable for devoted fans.

seed_books.title: The Wizard Knight
seed_books.author: Gene Wolfe
seed_books.why: Wolfe builds a Norse-inflected secondary world with the same mythic weight as Tolkien, layered with unreliable narration and meaning that deepens on every reread.

seed_books.title: The Tombs of Atuan
seed_books.author: Ursula K. Le Guin
seed_books.why: The second Earthsea book is often cited as Le Guin's most perfectly crafted — a claustrophobic, mythic novel about darkness, captivity and the cost of power.

seed_books.title: Titus Groan
seed_books.author: Mervyn Peake
seed_books.why: Peake's Gormenghast sequence creates one of literature's most complete and eccentric imaginary worlds — gothic, tragicomic and as inexhaustible as Middle-earth itself.

seed_books.title: The Shadow of the Torturer
seed_books.author: Gene Wolfe
seed_books.why: The opening volume of The Book of the New Sun is one of the most densely world-built novels in the canon — a dying Earth epic as mythically layered as Tolkien, written in a future-archaic English.
